item.title: Can the U.S. Rebuild Its Democracy After Trump?
item.description: The 250th anniversary of the United States represents more than just a major milestone – it is a defining moment for defending the country’s future. With Donald Trump’s authoritarian push eroding our democratic norms, the question is no longer about whether American democracy is under threat, but…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The 250th anniversary of the United States represents more than just a major milestone – it is a defining moment for defending the country’s future.

With Donald Trump’s authoritarian push eroding our democratic norms, the question is no longer about whether American democracy is under threat, but whether we are willing to fight for it.

So have we lost touch with the vision our founding fathers put forth? And is American democracy at its breaking point?

In this Substack Live conversation, Heather Cox Richardson, renowned historian and author of the Substack newsletter ‘Letters from an American’, spoke with Zeteo’s John Harwood about the state of American democracy and what we can learn from the past 250 years of our nation’s history.

She warns, “We’re on a knife’s edge between rising authoritarianism and the rebirth of American democracy and the question is: which one of us is going to cross the finish line first?”

This conversation was live on Zeteo.com and the Substack app, and both took questions from subscribers in the live audience. Feel free to share your thoughts on the discussion in the comments below, and be sure to keep an eye out for the next Substack Live!

Catch up on some of Zeteo’s latest stories:

Thank you for being a paid subscriber to Zeteo, your support helps keep this media movement alive. If you’d like to wear your support, don’t forget to check out

For any issues with your subscription, please email us at info@zeteonews.com.

item.title: ✈️ America’s Most Corrupt President Rides World’s Largest Metaphor for Corruption
item.description: On this day in 1964, President Lyndon B. Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act, a historic measure prohibiting racial discrimination in education and employment and outlawing racial segregation in public places. Good morning! Martin and Swin here. At Zeteo , we’ve spent time this week at Donald…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1964, President Lyndon B. Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act, a historic measure prohibiting racial discrimination in education and employment and outlawing racial segregation in public places.

Good morning! Martin and Swin here. At Zeteo, we’ve spent time this week at Donald Trump’s so-called Great American State Fair, on the National Mall in DC. We’ll have more to report soon (it’s going as well as you’ve heard, and yes, it does add another layer to the thesis, Trump Literally Can’t Do Anything Right), but until then, we need to talk about the president’s unbridled corruption. Since last year, multiple sources close to Trump have quipped that thanks solely to his second presidency, and his willingness to use it to line his and his family’s pockets, Trump will finally be able to say he’s an actual billionaire as opposed to a “fake” one. 😂 😭 The line might be funny… if it weren’t so immensely sad for a nominally democratic society.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we follow Trump’s chemtrails of corruption west to North Dakota, via his first flight on his new Air Force One, the totally normal, not staggeringly wrong gift from his friends in Qatar. We also take a trip back inside a presidential brain still absolutely furious about bestselling book Regime Change and consider other events from another insane day in DC and further abroad. Let’s dig in.

Famously, The Onion’s front page for April 16, 1912, about the sinking of the Titanic, reads: “World’s Largest Metaphor Hits Ice-Berg.” On Wednesday, Donald Trump was taking off rather than sinking, but the gag came to mind nonetheless. The world’s largest metaphor for the most corrupt U.S. president’s second administration – the new Air Force One, a $400 million gift from Qatar, a suitably emiratic symbol of this kakistocratic regime – made its maiden flight from Maryland to Medora, North Dakota, where Trump teed off celebrations of America’s 250th birthday at the Theodore Roosevelt library.

A reporter asked about financial disclosures that showed Trump’s staggering profit from the presidency: at least $2.2 billion in 2025, including about $1.4 billion from family crypto ventures, more than anyone else in the sector. “What message,” the reporter asked, “does this send to average Americans, especially those who may be struggling financially?”

Trump’s response is worth quoting in full, if only to show how he avoided the question by lying and blathering at length.

“I don’t get involved in my personal… we have funds that run my money. Well I’ve made a lot of money before I became president, and they invest my money, and I don’t talk to them. I never… I don’t even speak to them. So, I have many people. I don’t know what they call it, ‘closed accounts’ or something. You put your money in, and that’s it. I don’t talk to them, they’re big institutions, and they run it. But yeah, I’ve had a great career in business. I’ve had a great career. I don’t know if I’ve had a better career in politics or business, but I had a great career in business. And you know, you saw the cash and you report the different things, and what they do is, we gave it, I think it’s called a blind account, but they basically, they take it, and I purposely, I never speak to any of the people that run the money, but they’re big institutions, and they invest in whatever they invest.”

It was nonsense: horseshit d’oeuvres, if you will, for the speech Trump gave in the Dakotas, surrounded by cosplay Rough Riders. Trump has clearly, undoubtedly, obscenely profited from the presidency second time around. Consider just some of the many, many stories of insider dealing and ruthless profiteering.

On Sunday, the New York Times published a staggering story involving Trump, his sons, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick and his sons, and tungsten mining in Kazakhstan. By Wednesday, that story was in danger of being forgotten, swept away by the tide. Trump’s disclosures showed he made around $1.4 billion in stock purchases last year alone. Among such moves was a purchase of shares in a little-known rare-earth mining company which then received huge Pentagon investment, and an investment of more than $500,000 in Abbott Laboratories, two months before Trump’s own DOJ dropped an investigation into the company over contaminated baby formula potentially resulting in deaths.

Even amid all that, uniquely grotesque is the fact, sources tell Zeteo, that Trump has told confidants how “ stupid” he was not to cash in more on his first presidency. His initial run was a gloriously corrupt time for him and his lackeys, and yet term two has blown past it, constantly, in ways we once thought unthinkable.

The plane from the Qataris? Sources say Trump has privately told people it’s his, for personal use after he leaves office. All this talk of transferring the plane to his own presidential “library” is bullshit, and everyone around Trump knows it.

There is so much about the Trump-Vance era that should make any honest American vomit in shame. (Happy 250!) But the corruption is in a special category, in that it’s one of those things we were raised to believe couldn’t happen here. Is there corruption in the U.S.? Sure. Has there always been corruption? Absolutely. But this kind of kleptocracy by the head of state, his family, and their ruling party is what we read about in failed or failing states, where regimes rule with fear. Shame on us, for ever thinking our system was better than that!

Even Fox seems to have twigged that something is amiss. Anchor Bill Hemmer asked White House deputy press secretary Anna Kelly about the Kazakh tungsten deal and the crypto billions. Another evasive rant followed, claiming Americans elected Trump for his business success (daddy’s money and six bankruptcies notwithstanding), and a claim that Trump “has no control of those trades, he’s pretty busy as president, he’s not doing those trades from the Oval Office as he’s making decisions for our country.”

Hemmer didn’t look convinced. If even Fox is turning skeptical, is there a glimmer of hope?

Back to The Onion. The subhead to its Titanic story is: “Representation of Man’s Hubris Sinks in North Atlantic.” Trump’s new Air Force One stayed aloft on Wednesday, and thankful we all are for that, also because hundreds of billions in taxpayer dollars went into retrofitting the thing. But Trump has done us a favor. Whenever he uses his huge golden jet, boasts about his huge golden jet, or has his aides photographed enjoying his huge golden jet and its luxury fittings, the world will see his corruption bright and clear. Let’s just hope Trump can’t fleece the country completely before it finally finishes with him.

  • Hocus Potus:In Washington, Donald Trumpsaid“the denuclearization of Iran is moving along well,” adding: “They’ve had very good meetings, and we’ll see.”
  • No face time:In Doha, however, Trump envoys Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff and Iranian representatives eachheldtalks with intermediaries, not each other.
  • Costs of war:Absent strikes in or over the Strait, oil priceswereat pre-war levels. Average U.S. gasoline prices remained 30% up.
  • Chopper down:One U.S. airman was missing after a Sea Hawk helicopter crashed in the Arabian Sea. Three crew members were rescued. The U.S. Navysaidthere was no indication of hostile action.
  • Lebanon latest:Benjamin Netanyahu said he would keep Israeli forces in southern Lebanon “as long as necessary.” The Israeli prime minister spoke to mark the 20th anniversary of the 2006 war with Hezbollah, which resulted in around 1,200 Lebanese civilian deaths. Since March 2, the current war has killed nearly 4,300.

The president of the United States has been obsessed with Regime Change, Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan’s bestseller on his second administration, for months – to the point that earlier this year, as Zeteo first reported, he ordered senior officials to launch a massive leak hunt, to find and punish the two New York Times reporters’ sources. As we also told you, the leak hunt stalled largely because too many senior appointees charged with tracking down the leakers were in fact the leakers.

In recent days, our sources in the know have told us what Trump has been privately bitching about: In multiple “briefings” regarding the book and the authors’ ongoing media tour, the president has repeatedly asked advisers how the book has been selling, clearly hoping for low sales figures. On Wednesday, the publisher released the numbers and… boy, were they sure to piss off Trump, big time.

Further, sources familiar with the matter tell Zeteo Trump is particularly annoyed that he’s been seeing TV coverage of the book’s passages about his aide Natalie Harp. For years, he’s been especially sensitive about media coverage of his professional and personal relationship with Harp, and is still bitter about a leak of the so-called “love letters” Harp has written to him.

Anyway, it’s a long, long, long list of things that are slowly driving this Mad Emperor president, well, mad. But talk to anyone who knows what they’re about in the upper tiers of Trumplandia, and if they have a moment of candor, they’ll indeed tell you that Haberman, Swan, and their work are shredding his brain and his mood in ways the vast majority of things are not.

  • National bawl:Trump is “enraged” by evidence his “Great American State Fair” in DC is a sparsely attended flop, CNNreported, adding that officials worry Saturday’s July 4 celebration will be similarly awful. In North Dakota, Trumpsaidhe planned to “make a really long speech” in “107-degree” heat, “just to show that I can do anything.” What great news for people there to see thefireworks.
  • Surreality bites:Trump alsoappearedto speak to an AI Teddy Roosevelt as if it were alive. “It’s an honor to be with you today,” Trump said. Later, hetolda crowd he “had a conversation with Theodore Roosevelt” about the Panama Canal.
  • Lackey’s mission:As Trump muses over documents to keep when he leaves office, asreportedbyZeteo, the president has told his acting director of national intelligence, MAGA warrior Bill Pulte, to “declassify almost everything,” CNNsaid.
  • Kashing in:FBI Director Kash Patelfailedto properly disclose a six-figure purchase of stock in a Bitcoin-fueled company that has done DOJ business.
  • Man down:Senator Mitch McConnell, 84, was found unconscious at home in DC on June 14, Punchbowl Newsreported. The former GOP leader, who will retire this year, has not attended the Senate since. His staff has not detailed his condition.
  • Payback time:Colorado Governor Jared Polis, a Democrat,firedtwo officials who opposed his decision to free election denier, convicted felon, and Trump ally Tina Peters.
  • Values added:New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani willmarkthe 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a speech on Friday, from George Washington’s desk at City Hall, surrounded by immigrants.

In North Dakota on Wednesday, Donald Trump appeared to think he spoke to the real Theodore Roosevelt. Which historical figure did Hillary Clinton admit to talking to while she was first lady?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:An Israeli strike in northern Gaza killed three, the WAFA news agencysaid, as Israel continues to ignore the so-called “ceasefire.” Yesterday, Israeli Energy Minister Eli Cohensaidhis country now controls 70% of Gaza and plans to take over 100%. Today, Palestinians marked 1,000 days of Israel’s genocidal war.

  • 🇺🇦 🇷🇺

    ‘Night of horror’:Russiapoundedthe Ukrainian capital Kyiv overnight, killing at least 17 and injuring more than 90 others. Moscow said the deadly bombardment was in response to Ukraine’s attack on Russia’s oil facilities. Ukrainian officials describe it as a “night of horror.”
  • 🇻🇪 Disease warning:A week after two major earthquakes and with at least 2,295 dead, doctors in Venezuelawarnedthat untreated injuries and infectious disease will take more lives as the country struggles to cope. More than 11,000 people were injured in the quakes while thousands lost homes and access to clean water.

  • 🇺🇸🇨🇦🇲🇽

    Trade punt:The Trump administrationdecidednot to renew the USMCA, the trade pact with Canada and Mexico reached in the first Trump term, for another 16-year spell. It will be reviewed annually, instead.

  • 🇸🇩

    Grave charge:Amnesty Internationalsaidthree paramilitary commanders in Sudan oversaw war crimes during the siege and capture of el-Fasher in North Darfur last year. More than 6,000 were killed in just three days.


Donald Trump wants to give sons Donald Jr. and Eric “the Congressional Medal of Honor for something,” then give one to himself, and then… uh… yeah. You need to watch above and see what word the president of the United States uses to describe… well… just watch it for yourself.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Is This Mamdani’s 2029 Opponent? If So, LOL
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, does Zohran Mamdani need to take the threat of “Barstool conservatism” seriously ahead of his next…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, does Zohran Mamdani need to take the threat of “Barstool conservatism” seriously ahead of his next mayoral election? Also, Anderson Cooper just can’t seem to get away from Bari Weiss, and NPR really steps in it over a botched Supreme Court story.

Amid the current political freakout (from MAGA and establishment Democrats) over Zohran Mamdani’s role as kingmaker as anti-war progressives and democratic socialists rack up primary victories, the New York City mayor suddenly found himself a potential new challenger for 2029.

“I would love to run against [Mamdani]... If I was going to run, it would be here,” Dave Portnoy, the founder of the gleefully offensive and bro-tastic media empire Barstool Sports, declared on Jesse Watters’s Fox show Monday night.

The announcement from Portnoy, the self-anointed “El Presidente” who lost his defamation lawsuit against Business Insider over its stories about his alleged sexual misconduct, immediately drew online attention and a slew of media headlines, with conservative outlet Washington Examiner insisting Portnoy was “dead serious.” But is he?

During a Monday night event in Manhattan to celebrate and promote the release of his memoir, Cancel Me If You Can, he continued to tease a mayoral run while fearmongering about Mamdani. “I can’t wrap my brain around it, and I don’t know if it’s scary or I should be scared, or, like, I got to get in politics,” he said from the stage at 92nd Street Y, drawing cheers from his fans.

Portnoy, who details in his new book that his warm embrace of Donald Trump – which included a presidential interview in 2020 – created tension with his Barstool cohorts, further suggested that New Yorkers should embrace him for what he’s done for the city. “I am voting for Donald Trump. I don’t care if he’s a joke. I don’t care if he’s racist,” Portnoy proudly boasted in 2015. “I love the fact he says shit nobody else will say regardless of how ridiculous it is.”

“Unlike Mamdani, who’s never had a job. I’ve had a real job, and I’ve actually done a lot of good when I wasn’t doing it to run for office,” he declared on Monday evening, referencing his popular “One Bite Pizza Reviews” and the small business funds he helped set up during the COVID pandemic.

It was a similar argument to the one he made on Watters’s show, in which he pondered whether he could be victorious in New York – a city he doesn’t currently reside in but where his business is headquartered.

“I don’t know the demographics, whether they’re getting enough votes. There’s a lot of people who like me in New York City. I know that. I’ve done a lot of good in New York City when I wasn’t thinking about politics,” Portnoy, who has vowed to never hire any pro-Palestinian activists, told Watters.

Robert Silverman, an investigative journalist who has reported extensively on Portnoy and coined the term “Barstool conservatism,” told me that the self-described “El Presidente” would almost certainly not follow through on a run for mayor – noting that we’ve seen this before from Portnoy.

Indeed, back in 2013, Portnoy said he was “dead serious” about his Boston mayoral bid following his clashes with the city’s former mayor, Thomas Menino, over Barstool’s “Blackout Parties,” which were aimed at college students and sparked protests over accusations of underage drinking and misogyny. In the end, Portnoy failed to submit enough nomination signatures to qualify for the race.

“I think it’s a little sad that Dave Portnoy is trying to rework a 13-year-old bit about running for office when he couldn’t even bother to get into the race 13 years ago in his own hometown. That the idea that he’s trying, that he might even possibly consider it again, is kind of pathetic,” Silverman said of the Boston-bred provocateur.

“He’s not running for mayor,” Silverman noted. “Just to be clear, there is no instance in hell he will ever actually run. Will he do some dumb stunt press conference about it if he thinks it’ll get him dollar one? Yeah, very possibly.”

Though Silverman also noted that Portnoy’s core belief largely revolves around how “people should pay more attention to Dave Portnoy” and “his entire business model is dependent on people paying attention to Dave Portnoy,” the Barstool founder has been aggressively outspoken about Mamdani over the past year.

Portnoy threatened to pull Barstool from New York City following Mamdani’s victory last November, calling the popular progressive mayor a “communist” while outright saying, “I hate the guy.” Echoing the rest of MAGA media, Portnoy has continued to smear Mamdani as a “communist” on a regular basis and described him as a “professional actor” over the mayor’s gleeful reaction to the New York Knicks championship win.

“Look, he’s got a real feral instinct for sniffing out some viral trends, and I will absolutely credit him for that,” Silverman noted. "But for Portnoy, if the far-right on Twitter is freaking out about Mamdani, he will too."

Portnoy did not answer my questions about whether he was serious about a mayoral run. Instead, in response to my request for comment, he reverted to Barstool’s usual line of attack against Silverman, saying he went to a “clown college.” (Silverman graduated from L’École Internationale de Théâtre Jacques Lecoq in Paris, a post-college professional school of physical theater.)

Still, if there is one issue in which Portnoy appears to be genuinely passionate, it would be his support for Israel amid its genocide in Gaza, saying he feels there’s been a “definitive shift” toward antisemitism in recent years. Portnoy, who is Jewish, has also been the target of antisemitic incidents, such as a Mississippi State University student throwing coins at him while yelling a slur, or patrons at a Barstool-themed bar displaying a sign that read “Fuck the Jews.”

This has led an unhinged Portnoy to straight-up scream at his fellow Barstool colleagues for suggesting he was being sanctimonious and hypocritical for insisting that certain jokes about Jewish people were off limits – which went against the site’s longstanding anti-woke ‘anything goes’ ethos.

Of course, during the same June 2025 livestream in which Portnoy insisted that free speech should not apply to jokes he felt could spark antisemitic violence and said “you just can’t make jokes that incite hate,” he called for Greta Thunberg to be killed for taking part in a humanitarian mission to Gaza.

“I hope they hit a – fucking like a – missile on her boat. Knock that boat down. Greta or whatever her name is,” he snarled.

The New York Times dropped a buzzy story over the weekend about CNN bracing for the upcoming Paramount-Warner Bros. merger and the possibility of the 24-hour cable news network coming under the thumb of Bari Weiss, the anti-woke editor-in-chief of CBS News.

Besides reporting that CNN chief Mark Thompson has made it clear to Paramount that he will not share oversight of CNN with another executive and that Jake Tapper met with David Ellison – the Trump-friendly owner of Paramount – this past spring, the Times revealed that Anderson Cooper does not want to work for Weiss.

Cooper, having grown frustrated with Weiss’s management style, essentially gave her the middle finger earlier this year when he decided to leave ‘60 Minutes’ as a correspondent after two decades. Weiss, who also desperately courted Cooper to anchor ‘CBS Evening News,’ was infuriated over Cooper’s farewell message following his final episode of ‘60 Minutes.’

Sources I’ve spoken to have confirmed that Cooper – CNN’s unquestioned biggest star – would almost certainly consider packing it up if Weiss were to have any editorial leadership role at the network. “He fled from Bari once, and now, he can’t run far enough away,” one network reporter told Zeteo.

A separate CNN staffer suggested that Cooper would “probably leave if she comes aboard,” adding that they “just couldn’t see him sticking around” under Weiss.

The same source added that Cooper has also “expressed some angst about” the looming situation. Ellison has remained supportive of Weiss despite her chaotic reign atop CBS News, which has included blowing up ‘60 Minutes’ and journalists accusing her of editorial meddling.

“He already left one job working for her. No one I’ve spoken to wants to work for Bari. Myself included,” another CNN producer told me.

Some CNN veterans have already decided to cut bait now rather than wait for the merger – which is still facing some regulatory hurdles – to be finalized. Status reported on Tuesday night that correspondent Paula Reid will exit CNN once her contract ends in a few months, stating that she “has privately expressed concern about the sweeping changes implemented at” CBS News since Ellison took over. Kara Swisher has also made it known that she will ditch CNN once the Paramount deal is done.

CNN and a spokesperson for Cooper declined to comment.

How the hell did this happen?

For a brief moment on Tuesday, the political world was rocked by the sudden news that Supreme Court Justice Samuel Alito had announced his retirement following the final day of the high court’s session. The fact that the story came from NPR correspondent Nina Totenberg, perhaps the most prominent Supreme Court chronicler, lent it even more gravitas.

Instead, the 1,200-word report – which was obviously pre-written in anticipation of such an announcement – was quickly taken down and retracted after being live for just five minutes. And the explanations from both NPR’s editors and Totenberg herself have only raised more questions and eyebrows.

After NPR editor-in-chief Tommy Evans said Totenberg “incorrectly reported” the news due to a “misunderstanding,” Kelly McBride – the organization’s public editor – explained in a blog post how the fatal error occurred. Yet, it came out before Totenberg appeared on ‘All Things Considered’ to give her side of things, and ended up having to be corrected itself as McBride initially believed that Totenberg heard the supposed court announcement herself.

Calling it a “rookie mistake,” Totenberg – who also personally apologized to Alito – revealed that she asked a bystander why everyone wasn’t leaving the court at the end of the session and was told that Chief Justice John Roberts was making “retirement announcements,” leading her to jump to the conclusion that it was about Alito. (That’s right, she wasn’t even in the room when the supposed announcement was made!) She then called up NPR Executive Editor Krishnadev Calamur about what she heard, who grabbed her pre-write and published it right away.

Calamur said it was Totenberg’s legendary status that prompted him to rush the story out and trigger this worst-case scenario for the outlet. “This sort of shit should not happen,” Calamur added.

Many media observers still feel that there is more to this story than what NPR and Totenberg are saying publicly. “The explanation about NPR’s bogus ‘Justice Samuel Alito retires’ story just doesn’t add up,” CNN’s Brian Stelter wrote this morning, wondering why Totenberg “automatically believed what she heard secondhand” or NPR published without other sourcing.

With Fox intrepidly trying to add a positive spin to Trump’s sparsely attended Great American State Fair, which The Guardian likened to “White Star Line claiming people had enjoyed a very pleasant four days at sea before the Titanic hit that iceberg,” the conservative network’s live dispatches from the National Mall have only served to provide ammunition for online mockery of the event. With that in mind, check out our supercut of Fox’s lowlights from the fair – and make sure to stick around for the debut of “Pistachio Ice.”

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘ Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: ‘This One!’: Trump Is Already Planning To Steal Classified Docs Again
item.description: Remember that open-and-shut federal criminal case where Donald Trump clearly committed serious crimes, but managed to escape justice thanks to the right-wing supermajority he built on the Supreme Court? No, not the case over Jan. 6 and his efforts to overturn the election he lost – the other…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Remember that open-and-shut federal criminal case where Donald Trump clearly committed serious crimes, but managed to escape justice thanks to the right-wing supermajority he built on the Supreme Court?

No, not the case over Jan. 6 and his efforts to overturn the election he lost – the other federal case, the one about how he stole a bunch of classified government documents and stored some next to his toilet at Mar-a-Lago, and refused to give them back to the U.S. government after he left office the first time. Remember? Well, the president would like to do it again, minus the part where anyone attempts to hold him accountable.

President Trump is already angling to retain whichever currently classified or top secret government documents he wants after he leaves the White House again, two sources familiar with the matter and another two briefed on it tell Zeteo. In some cases, he’s made comments suggesting he’s already picked out files to keep.

“This one would be good!” he’s joked (or, “joked”) while holding a document up in the Oval Office within the past year, according to one of the sources who was present for it.

And this time, his government, including his Department of Justice, is really willing to help him do it – or at least lay the groundwork for him to abscond with classified government property once again.

In some cases, the sources tell Zeteo, Trump has talked about making certain classified docs available for his presidential library. Other times, he’s talking about how he wants to “take them” to Mar-a-Lago for his personal collection in his next post-presidency. In some of these instances, legal or political advisers have reminded him that if he wants to do that, remember to declassify them before departing the White House.

Trump’s continued desire to hoard, for the rest of his life, potentially sensitive U.S. documents further underscores the abject lawlessness of the second Trump administration – and also his desire to corruptly wield federal power to his own personal ends, including for score-settling and nursed grudges of the past. In this instance, it’s partly about giving another middle finger to the federal case against him. He’s nursed this specific grudge for years; not long after his Florida home of Mar-a-Lago was raided by the feds in 2022, he kept telling his lawyers and advisers to get “my” classified documents back.

The White House did not respond to Zeteo’s request for comment on this story.

During his first term, Trump was known to hoard documents in bankers boxes in his bedroom. “First-term aides would privately refer to them as his ‘Beautiful Mind’ boxes, a sardonic reference to the biopic of the schizophrenic math prodigy John Nash,” Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan write in their recent book Regime Change, adding: “The boxes were sometimes stacked so high that they left impressions in the carpet when moved.”

This detail came up in the classified documents case, with a Trump employee shown texting a colleague that “anything that’s not the beautiful mind paper boxes can definitely go to storage.” The case ended up getting tossed by Judge Aileen Cannon, one of Trump’s favorite appointments, after the Supreme Court gifted the president expansive immunity from prosecution for all official acts.

According to Swan and Haberman, with the ‘Beautiful Mind’ boxes becoming “a symbol of one of Trump’s two federal indictments,” the president’s advisers have stopped hauling documents around the West Wing in boxes, carrying them around loose instead. Additionally, they write, the president has turned Jared Kushner’s old office “into an all-purpose hoarder space packed with papers, paintings, tchotchkes, MAGA paraphernalia, and various gifts he couldn’t bear to throw away.”

Under the Presidential Records Act, White House records belong to the public, and they’re meant to be transferred to the National Archives to be retained. After his first term, Trump (in)famously refused to comply with the act; instead, he and his team claimed that he was entitled to hold onto the documents under the law. The resulting fiasco led to one of Special Counsel Jack Smith’s criminal cases against the then-former U.S. president.

In his post-presidency, Trump plotted to purge the National Archives of any staff who may have been involved in the effort to recover the classified documents Trump was hoarding. And once he became president again, Trump moved to fire the U.S. Archivist, and compiled a list of other potential names to purge at the archives.

Starting early this year, lawyers and other sources in the U.S. government began alerting Zeteo about the Trump administration’s commitments to wrecking the Presidential Records Act – for this and other reasons.

In April, the Office of Legal Counsel in Trump’s Justice Department issued a memorandum declaring the Presidential Records Act unconstitutional, writing that “the president need not further comply with its dictates.”

“The PRA… unconstitutionally intrudes on the independence and autonomy of the president guaranteed by Article II,” T. Elliot Gaiser, chief at the Office of Legal Counsel, stated, dubiously. “The act establishes a permanent and burdensome regime of congressional regulation of the presidency untethered from any valid and identifiable legislative purpose.”

item.title: AIPAC Defeated Again – This Time in Colorado
item.description: On this day in 1972, the first standalone issue of ‘Ms.’ magazine was published. Founded by Gloria Steinem, the groundbreaking feminist title is still in print. Good morning, friends. Prem here, with two stories for you that together serve to illustrate how the monstrosity of the old world may at…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1972, the first standalone issue of ‘Ms.’ magazine was published. Founded by Gloria Steinem, the groundbreaking feminist title is still in print.

Good morning, friends. Prem here, with two stories for you that together serve to illustrate how the monstrosity of the old world may at last be proving too much to bear. We all have our limits. A new world is no longer struggling to be born. Here it comes. To that end, prepare for elected Republicans to grow even more insane and ‘Reasonable Democrats’ to spiral even more, as socialism spreads from sea to shining sea in the United States!

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we consider another momentous win for the left, this time in Colorado; a determined Democratic effort to hold President Donald Trump to account over his preposterous Board of Peace and its plans for Gaza; and the Supreme Court turns Trump down on birthright citizenship, though not without four justices showing they don’t believe in… the Constitution. Let’s get to it.

A democratic socialist born in 1997 has unseated a veteran Democrat who was first elected to Congress in… 1997.

Last night, 29-year-old Melat Kiros beat 30-year incumbent Diana DeGette, 68, in the Democratic primary for Colorado’s first congressional district in Denver.

Kiros’s victory in the Mile High City comes just one week after a Zohran Mamdani-backed trio of democratic socialists – Claire Valdez, Darializa Avila Chevalier, and Brad Lander – strung together three primary wins in New York City. In total, three Democratic incumbents have been usurped by challengers from the left in just the past week.

The Colorado election is another notch in the belt for the democratic socialist movement – showing promise beyond the well-oiled machine that is the New York City chapter of the Democratic Socialists of America.

New York was not a one-off, says Bhavik Lathia, a Democratic strategist and spokesman for the Progressive Change Campaign Committee. “Kiros’s win validates that the same political energy is moving across the country — from Maine to New York to Pennsylvania to Colorado. The common thread is voters looking for outsider candidates who will fight Trump and corporate power with equal clarity,” he tells Zeteo. “The socialist sweep in New York showed that voters are hungry for a Democratic Party willing to change. Kiros’ win shows that hunger is national.”

AIPAC will be feeling super disappointed this morning. Again.

Just months ago, DeGette was leading Kiros by more than 30 points. Kiros had little name ID relative to the three-decade incumbent. Then, as Kiros surged, so did funded opposition against her. Super PACs linked to the real estate lobby, the pro-Israel group AIPAC, and the Democratic establishment – and some that came out of nowhere, with little disclosure – all jumped into the race in support of DeGette, and against Kiros.

Still, as is increasingly the case, millions in big money were not enough to best the DSA-powered campaign.

Kiros, an attorney born in Ethiopia who lost her job with a corporate law firm in New York after writing an article defending pro-Palestine college students, now joins an increasingly long list of outsider Democrats set to join Congress in November and challenge the party establishment.

Like Avila Chevalier and Valdez, Kiros is not committing to supporting Hakeem Jeffries as leader – instead demanding concessions on popular proposals with cross-partisan appeal, including Medicare for All, “ending corporate capture” of politics, and an arms embargo on Israel.

Kiros’s race wasn’t the only one in which pro-Israel forces lost. In Colorado’s 8th congressional district – a swing seat held by the GOP – Democratic Majority for Israel endorsee Shannon Bird lost big.

Bird, like DeGette, received support from a super PAC called Pro-Choice Majority Action, which was bankrolled through a daisy chain of groups whose funding traces back to AIPAC.

Elsewhere, Senator John Hickenlooper bested a primary bid from Working Families Party-backed progressive challenger, state Senator Julie Gonzales. In the governor’s race, Senator Michael Bennet was handily defeated by Attorney General Phil Weiser, who ran an anti-corporate campaign.

It is Kiros’s victory that is of particular significance.

In the wake of the New York City socialist sweep, Republicans and old guard Democrats closed ranks, all setting their sights on the bubbling political movement.

A small group of centrist Democratic members and candidates released a parochial pledge entitled the “Promise to America,” which glorified capitalism, demonized socialism, and vomited other vague centrist axioms about Getting Stuff Done and being Responsible.

House Speaker Mike Johnson, meanwhile, smugly listed off priorities for the democratic socialist movement, thinking the laundry list would sound absurd. Instead, his remarks were turned into a DSA hype video.

The goalposts have continued to move, again and again.

Mamdani would never win.

Fine, he won, but he won’t accomplish anything.

Fine, but his new endorsed candidates won’t win, too.

Fine, but that success can’t be replicated outside of New York City.

What’s next?

  • Talks… for now:Trump has been briefed about a return to all-out war but chosen diplomacy, theWall Street Journalreported. Envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushnerwentto Doha to meet with mediators.
  • Cash boost:A U.S. official confirmed to the AP that Qatar will unfreeze $6 billion in Iranian assets,sayingit would be used to buy food for the people of Iran.
  • Strait to the point:On state TV, Iranian Speaker Mohammed Bagher Ghalibafsaid: “We are pursuing dialogue, but if the dialogue is not implemented, we are also prepared for war and will respond accordingly.” He alsosaidthe sovereignty of the Strait of Hormuz “lies with Iran and Oman, and traffic in the Strait is subject to arrangements determined by Iran.”
  • Gas demand:As oil prices fell, thanks to the end for now of strikes over the Strait, Trumpdemandedretailers lower gas prices at U.S. pumps.
  • Lebanon latest:Benjamin NetanyahuvisitedIsraeli soldiers in Lebanese territory, vowing not to pull troops out until Hezbollah is defeated. More than 4,000 Lebanese have been killed and more than 1 million displaced since March.
  • Voted down:House Democrats helped the GOPkilla Lebanon war powers vote championed by Rep. Rashida Tlaib. The number of Democrats opposed to the move was vastly down on the first time Tlaib tried it, though, as frustration with Israel grows.

After Hillary Clinton endorsed President Donald Trump’s so-called “Board of Peace,” progressives including Clinton’s old opponent, Bernie Sanders, are launching an inquiry into the dystopian Gaza initiative, Zeteo can reveal.

Illinois Rep. Delia Ramirez is the lead signatory on a letter to the Trump State Department, demanding answers on the board’s planned “International Stabilization Force”: how it is being funded, whether Trump has absolute authority over those funds, what the U.S. is doing to compel Israel to comply with the “peace plan,” and more.

The questions are directed chiefly at Secretary of State Marco Rubio.

Sanders and Reps. Rashida Tlaib, Andre Carson, Ayanna Pressley, Hank Johnson, Summer Lee, Chuy Garcia, Al Green, and Ilhan Omar signed the letter.

“Congress has yet to receive clear answers from the Administration as to how the ‘Board of Peace’ – created without congressional authorization or appropriation, containing war criminals and authoritarians, and operating entirely outside of traditional international governing structures – will meaningfully deliver peace for Palestinians and Israelis,” the letter reads.

It continues: “President Trump serves as chairman for life in his personal capacity, not as President, with absolute veto power over all decisions… The Board has yet to demonstrate it is anything but a corrupt institution more interested in securing billions in real estate deals for Trump’s family and allies than adhering to the rule of law and alleviating suffering for millions of civilians.”

The Board of Peace has been hit with allegations of corruption and mismanagement since its conception: claims that it was created not to provide peace to over 2 million Palestinians as much as to provide a cash cow for the ghouls who have terrorized such vulnerable people.

Nations buy membership, contributing to a fund controlled by Trump. The project’s vision includes a development of Gaza hardly concerned with the desires of the Palestinian people, as much as meeting the needs of real estate developers. And it’s led by the pro-Israel warmonger and money-hungry U.S. president himself, who has even suggested his board could replace the United Nations.

Days ago, The Guardian revealed the board is planning to give members sweeping legal immunity regarding any criminality they may be charged with in Gaza. Members who would gain such immunity include Trump’s son-in-law, Jared Kushner; his special envoy to the Middle East, Steve Witkoff; his chief of staff, Susie Wiles; his Secretary of State, Rubio; and former British Prime Minister Tony Blair.

The board also plans to allow itself to take property in Gaza “free of charge,” for the “accomplishment” of its “missions.” You need not stretch your mind to imagine how the Trump cabal may use such language, for instance, to seize buildings that look favorable for the “mission” of building a privatized Gaza riviera!

  • Rulings class:The Supreme Court handed Trump one big defeat, upholding birthright citizenship (if by a 5-4 vote thatshowedJustices Alito, Thomas, Gorsuch, and Kavanaugh think the Constitution means nothing), and two big wins:endingrestrictions on how much parties can spend on candidates, andupholdingbans on trans athletes in high-school sports.
  • Retiring type:NPR said hard-right Justice Samuel Alito would retire, thenretractedthe story. Veteran reporter Nina Totenbergsaidshe misheard a mention that Chief Justice John Roberts was announcing retirements and rushed to publish, “the worst professional mistake of my more than 50 years in journalism.”
  • Daily disgraces:In the Oval Office, Trumpmetelection denier and felon Tina Peters. TheWashington Postreportedthe administration sidestepped procedure to award a $500 million no-bid contract for Trump’s White House Ballroom. Trump’s financialdisclosuresshowedmore than $2 billionin income, $1.4 billion from crypto. Melania raked in more than $17 million, most thanks to her Amazon doc.
  • Pay up:After the Supreme Court refused to take up Trump’s appeal against his civil penalty for sexually abusing E Jean Carroll, Carrollmovedto collect $5.8 million.
  • Pyongyang on the Potomac:MassiveJuly 4 National Mall fireworks will start at 11pm or later after Trump speaks and his new Air Force One, gifted by Qatar, is part of a flypast, Axiosreported. DC parents and pet owners, unite and take over.
  • House divided:Speaker Mike Johnsoncalledan early recess, unable to control GOP hardliners demanding progress on the SAVE Act “election security” measure Trump wants but the Senate can’t pass. Doubly embarrassing for Johnson, GOP defections ensured the failure of a “rule,” a measure to set up further votes.
  • Unconventional thinking:Trumpannounceda Republican “midterm convention” in Dallas in September. A key Democratic midterm target, New Jersey GOP Rep. Tom Kean,explainedthe recent disappearance that saw him miss more than 100 votes, saying he had been hospitalized with depression.
  • Senate races:Democrat James Talarico and MAGA scandal magnet (andanti-IVF zealot) Ken Paxton are tied on 47% in Texas, aNew York Times/Siena College pollshowed. In Maine, Graham Platner, the Democratic candidate,appealedfor outside help to match spending backing GOP incumbent Susan Collins.

Some people know Justice Samuel Alito as “Strip Search Sammy.” Why?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Al Jazeera publishedfootageof a man in Gaza surviving a “double-tap” car strike that killed three. “Double-taps,” in which follow-up strikes target survivors and often those who rushed to the scene of the first strike, are potential war crimes. At the UN, Secretary-General Antonio ​Guterresurgedcountries to cover a $100 million gap in funding for the UN Palestinian ‌refugee agency, UNRWA.

  • 🇲🇨🇺🇦

    Monaco blast:A Ukrainian construction tycoon with ties to Russia wasinjuredin an explosion at an apartment building.
  • 🇻🇪 Quake toll:Aid groupswarnedthat Venezuela’s public health system is near collapse after two major earthquakes last week. The government said more than 1,900 deaths were confirmed, but the toll was expected to be much higher.
  • 🇵🇰 Deadly collapse:Fourteen children aged between 5 and 16 werekilledin Lahore, Pakistan, when the roof of a tutoring center collapsed.
  • 🇨🇩🇺🇬 Ebola update:The UN said the Ebola outbreak centered on DR Congo could cost $3.6 billion and hundreds of thousands of jobs. Theoutbreakhas infected 1,307 and ​killed 377 in DR Congo, with a smaller number of cases in Uganda.


Stephen Miller really is a revolting, unnerving little ghoul. Even Jesse Watters of Fox ends up laughing uneasily here.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

Sanders and Reps. Rashida Tlaib, Andre Carson, Ayanna Pressley, Hank Johnson, Summer Lee, Chuy Garcia, Al Green, and Ilhan Omar signed the letter.

“Congress has yet to receive clear answers from the Administration as to how the ‘Board of Peace’ – created without congressional authorization or appropriation, containing war criminals and authoritarians, and operating entirely outside of traditional international governing structures – will meaningfully deliver peace for Palestinians and Israelis,” the letter reads.

It continues: “President Trump serves as chairman for life in his personal capacity, not as President, with absolute veto power over all decisions… The Board has yet to demonstrate it is anything but a corrupt institution more interested in securing billions in real estate deals for Trump’s family and allies than adhering to the rule of law and alleviating suffering for millions of civilians.”

The Board of Peace has been hit with allegations of corruption and mismanagement since its conception: claims that it was created not to provide peace to over 2 million Palestinians as much as to provide a cash cow for the ghouls who have terrorized such vulnerable people.

Nations buy membership, contributing to a fund controlled by Trump. The project’s vision includes a development of Gaza hardly concerned with the desires of the Palestinian people, as much as meeting the needs of real estate developers. And it’s led by the pro-Israel warmonger and money-hungry U.S. president himself, who has even suggested his board could replace the United Nations.

Days ago, The Guardian revealed the board is planning to give members sweeping legal immunity regarding any criminality they may be charged with in Gaza. Members who would gain such immunity include Trump’s son-in-law, Jared Kushner; his special envoy to the Middle East, Steve Witkoff; his chief of staff, Susie Wiles; his Secretary of State, Rubio; and former British Prime Minister Tony Blair.

The board also plans to allow itself to take property in Gaza “free of charge,” for the “accomplishment” of its “missions.” You need not stretch your mind to imagine how the Trump cabal may use such language, for instance, to seize buildings that look favorable for the “mission” of building a privatized Gaza riviera!

  • Rulings class:The Supreme Court handed Trump one big defeat, upholding birthright citizenship (if by a 5-4 vote thatshowedJustices Alito, Thomas, Gorsuch, and Kavanaugh think the Constitution means nothing), and two big wins:endingrestrictions on how much parties can spend on candidates, andupholdingbans on trans athletes in high-school sports.
  • Retiring type:NPR said hard-right Justice Samuel Alito would retire, thenretractedthe story. Veteran reporter Nina Totenbergsaidshe misheard a mention that Chief Justice John Roberts was announcing retirements and rushed to publish, “the worst professional mistake of my more than 50 years in journalism.”
  • Daily disgraces:In the Oval Office, Trumpmetelection denier and felon Tina Peters. TheWashington Postreportedthe administration sidestepped procedure to award a $500 million no-bid contract for Trump’s White House Ballroom. Trump’s financialdisclosuresshowedmore than $2 billionin income, $1.4 billion from crypto. Melania raked in more than $17 million, most thanks to her Amazon doc.
  • Pay up:After the Supreme Court refused to take up Trump’s appeal against his civil penalty for sexually abusing E Jean Carroll, Carrollmovedto collect $5.8 million.
  • Pyongyang on the Potomac:MassiveJuly 4 National Mall fireworks will start at 11pm or later after Trump speaks and his new Air Force One, gifted by Qatar, is part of a flypast, Axiosreported. DC parents and pet owners, unite and take over.
  • House divided:Speaker Mike Johnsoncalledan early recess, unable to control GOP hardliners demanding progress on the SAVE Act “election security” measure Trump wants but the Senate can’t pass. Doubly embarrassing for Johnson, GOP defections ensured the failure of a “rule,” a measure to set up further votes.
  • Unconventional thinking:Trumpannounceda Republican “midterm convention” in Dallas in September. A key Democratic midterm target, New Jersey GOP Rep. Tom Kean,explainedthe recent disappearance that saw him miss more than 100 votes, saying he had been hospitalized with depression.
  • Senate races:Democrat James Talarico and MAGA scandal magnet (andanti-IVF zealot) Ken Paxton are tied on 47% in Texas, aNew York Times/Siena College pollshowed. In Maine, Graham Platner, the Democratic candidate,appealedfor outside help to match spending backing GOP incumbent Susan Collins.

Some people know Justice Samuel Alito as “Strip Search Sammy.” Why?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Al Jazeera publishedfootageof a man in Gaza surviving a “double-tap” car strike that killed three. “Double-taps,” in which follow-up strikes target survivors and often those who rushed to the scene of the first strike, are potential war crimes. At the UN, Secretary-General Antonio ​Guterresurgedcountries to cover a $100 million gap in funding for the UN Palestinian ‌refugee agency, UNRWA.

  • 🇲🇨🇺🇦

    Monaco blast:A Ukrainian construction tycoon with ties to Russia wasinjuredin an explosion at an apartment building.
  • 🇻🇪 Quake toll:Aid groupswarnedthat Venezuela’s public health system is near collapse after two major earthquakes last week. The government said more than 1,900 deaths were confirmed, but the toll was expected to be much higher.
  • 🇵🇰 Deadly collapse:Fourteen children aged between 5 and 16 werekilledin Lahore, Pakistan, when the roof of a tutoring center collapsed.
  • 🇨🇩🇺🇬 Ebola update:The UN said the Ebola outbreak centered on DR Congo could cost $3.6 billion and hundreds of thousands of jobs. Theoutbreakhas infected 1,307 and ​killed 377 in DR Congo, with a smaller number of cases in Uganda.


Stephen Miller really is a revolting, unnerving little ghoul. Even Jesse Watters of Fox ends up laughing uneasily here.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: Trump Wants YOUR Medical Data for His Drug War
item.description: The Trump administration’s new drug strategy does more than escalate the war on fentanyl. It sketches the architecture for a national surveillance system built from some of Americans’ most personal data: prescriptions, toxicology results, wastewater, electronic health records, license plate scans,…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The Trump administration’s new drug strategy does more than escalate the war on fentanyl. It sketches the architecture for a national surveillance system built from some of Americans’ most personal data: prescriptions, toxicology results, wastewater, electronic health records, license plate scans, and law enforcement intelligence.

The plans are laid out in the 2026 National Drug Control Strategy, a 195-page blueprint released in early May. Drug Czar Sara Carter announced it on Sean Hannity’s Fox show, calling it “a battle cry.” The document promises to “fully resource and empower” Homeland Security Task Forces, “leverage advanced technology and Artificial Intelligence,” and “modernize and integrate public safety and public health data collection,” applying “advanced data science methods” to “toxicology results, wastewater analysis, electronic health records, and law enforcement seizures.”

The strategy describes a surveillance architecture but never addresses how it may or may not be used. Nowhere does the strategy mention HIPAA. Nowhere does it say how patient privacy would be protected. Nowhere does it explain what limits would prevent a system built for fentanyl from being used to monitor abortion, pregnancy, gender transition, methadone treatment, protest activity, or any other behavior a future administration decides to target.

To law enforcement veterans, the appeal of a system that fuses health data, law enforcement intelligence, and modern analytics into a single system is obvious. Derek Maltz, a former acting DEA administrator who spent a decade running the agency’s Special Operations Division, has spent years arguing that cartels, Chinese chemical brokers, money laundering networks, and synthetic drug suppliers have outpaced a fragmented federal response.

“If you wanna connect the dots, you have to collect the dots,” Maltz told me.

Maltz talks about families whose children died from fentanyl, the collages of dead kids he takes to public events, and the parents who told him they never got the warning that might have saved their child. He describes his work as “not a red or a blue issue,” but “red, white, and blue.” The threat is real: Chinese chemical networks linked to Mexican cartels feed a synthetic-drug supply that kills tens of thousands of Americans a year. Maltz argues that agencies need integrated data and modern tools to keep pace with networks that already operate across borders, banks, chemicals, and encrypted platforms.

Even so, he conceded the limits of enforcement. “We are not going to seize our way out of this problem,” he said. “We are not going to arrest our way out of this problem.”

That is what makes the strategy’s surveillance ambitions so consequential. Fentanyl is deadly, and law enforcement does need better tools. The harder question is what happens when public health infrastructure becomes law enforcement infrastructure, and the government builds a machine with no clear oversight.

Much of the machinery already exists. Every state runs a prescription drug database. Police use national license plate reader networks. Wastewater surveillance expanded during COVID. Risk scores sit atop prescription records, and health data is already used in criminal cases.

The 2026 strategy doesn’t invent these systems from scratch. It connects them, which is the more consequential move.

Every time a doctor prescribes a controlled substance – painkillers, stimulants, anti-anxiety medication, certain hormones – that prescription goes into a state-run database called a Prescription Drug Monitoring Program (PDMP). Every state has one. These databases are not protected by HIPAA. Federal drug agents can access any state’s database by filing a subpoena that requires no judicial review.

“This is a wild, wild west,” said Jennifer Oliva, a law professor at Indiana University who has spent a decade studying prescription drug databases.

The federal government helped build these systems through grants from the Department of Justice. The DEA could not collect prescriber-level data on its own, so it funded states to build the databases. The opioid crisis made that politically easy. But the databases were never limited to opioids.

“There’s no reason to think that the same mechanisms in place to track and respond to potential opioid misuse can’t be turned to other drugs,” said Corey Davis, a senior attorney at the Network for Public Health Law. And there is “decent evidence that PDMPs do move people from prescription opioid misuse to misuse of street opioids, which is a net negative.” The system that was supposed to solve the opioid crisis made it worse. The same architecture is now being expanded.

States can also expand prescription-monitoring databases beyond opioids through controlled-substance scheduling, “drugs of concern” categories, or similar reporting rules. Others formally reschedule drugs under state law. Louisiana went further in 2024, becoming the first state to classify mifepristone and misoprostol as Schedule IV controlled dangerous substances, placing abortion medications inside the state’s controlled-substances monitoring regimen. Texas and Indiana lawmakers have introduced similar reclassification proposals, and Idaho has reportedly been considering similar tracking measures.

Testosterone is already federally controlled as a Schedule III anabolic steroid and has been tracked through controlled-substance reporting since anabolic steroids were added to the Controlled Substances Act in 1990. You cannot tell, from a database entry, whether testosterone is prescribed for low hormone levels or for gender transition. You cannot tell whether mifepristone is prescribed for an abortion or a miscarriage.

Craig Konnoth, a law professor at the University of Virginia, called this “a clear example of targeting a vulnerable minority.” Asked whether there is legal recourse, he said: “Unfortunately, in my opinion, no.”

A company called Bamboo Health runs a platform called NarxCare on top of these prescription databases. It assigns patients risk scores based on factors such as how they pay, whether they have insurance, and how far they travel to the pharmacy. No state requires doctors to tell patients the system exists. Patients cannot see their scores.

The 2026 drug strategy proposes going further: “applying predictive analytics to electronic health records to identify patients at high risk of overdose.”

That may sound clinical. But Charlotte Tschider, a health AI scholar at Loyola Chicago, said the “current regulatory framework does not distinguish between the use of AI for helping patients and enforcement.” Nothing in the law stops a system built to identify overdose risk from being repurposed to flag who is getting an abortion pill or hormone therapy.

The records that AI would analyze are not the clean clinical documents most people imagine. They are billing tools. Dr. Mishka Terplan, an OB/GYN and addiction medicine specialist, gave an example: a patient has one positive drug test at her first prenatal visit and nothing after. The billing code “drug abuse complicating pregnancy” gets attached to her record anyway. “Complicating” just means it happened at the same time, not that drugs caused a problem.

But in court, judges may read it as proof.

“The chart says drug abuse complicating pregnancy in the third trimester, therefore you must be lying,” Terplan said. “And the judge agrees.”

The strategy proposes AI analytics applied to these records.

Terplan said his patients are “legitimately reluctant to disclose and may sometimes, not always, forego care or delay care.” What he tells them now: “I can’t ensure protection of that information any longer.”

The danger is not hypothetical. Medical information already moves from healthcare into the criminal legal system. According to Pregnancy Justice, more than 64% of pregnancy criminalization cases involve information flowing from healthcare settings into the legal system.

In late December 2025, Alexia Moore went to a Georgia emergency room after a medical crisis. She had obtained abortion pills from an online provider. Hospital staff contacted the police. In March, she was charged with murder, based entirely on medical records, toxicology results, and statements taken at her bedside. No surveillance technology was involved.

Karen Thompson, Pregnancy Justice’s legal director, said: “The point of this is to terrify people. The point of this has no scientific function.”

Now add to that existing pipeline more data, more automation, more scoring, and more law enforcement integration.

During the COVID pandemic, public health agencies built the National Wastewater Surveillance System to test sewage for the virus. That funding is now drying up. The drug strategy proposes to fill the gap with law enforcement money, to “implement wastewater testing for the first time at a national scale.” On ‘Hannity,’ Carter confirmed: “We’re doing wastewater testing right now across the nation.”

Mariana Matus, CEO of Biobot Analytics, the largest commercial wastewater testing company, confirmed that “Biobot uses the same wastewater collection infrastructure for both pathogen and drug trend surveillance.”

The technology can detect HIV prevention drugs, testosterone, psychiatric medications, and abortion pills. Matus confirmed her company received inquiries about testing for abortion-related drugs from “local government agents.” She would not say which ones. “We have no plans to develop abortifacient testing,” she said.

But when asked about connections between Biobot’s lead venture funder – Valor Equity Partners, run by Antonio Gracias, a major Trump donor and former DOGE official – and Biobot’s January 2026 Office of National Drug Control Policy wastewater intelligence contract, she did not answer. Instead, she volunteered that she “would welcome the opportunity to educate” law enforcement on integrating wastewater intelligence.

Rolf Halden, a scientist at Arizona State University who built the first U.S. citywide wastewater drug monitoring program, independently confirmed that “almost any medication or its metabolites can be monitored in wastewater.” His lab also received inquiries about testing for abortion drugs.

What protects individuals from identification, he said, “are not regulatory barriers but the high cost at this point in time.”

The CDC’s National Wastewater Surveillance System, built during the pandemic, runs on Palantir’s Foundry platform under a $443 million contract. The CDC has said it will have no funding to sustain the program beyond September 30, 2026. Meanwhile, there are bills in several states that propose requiring wastewater testing for abortion-related drugs.

Natalie Ram and Jessica Roberts, law professors at the University of Maryland and Emory, warned that wastewater monitoring for mifepristone, “if successful, would create a roadmap for surveilling, suppressing, and sanctioning other politically contested medical care.”

Prescription databases show what people are prescribed. Electronic health records show what clinicians document and bill. Wastewater can show what communities consume or excrete. License plate readers show where people go.

The strategy also encompasses six overlapping federal license plate reader networks that Congress never authorized. What stops them from tracking someone driving to an abortion clinic or a methadone program?

“Under the current way that law enforcement uses these systems, absolutely nothing stops that kind of abuse,” said Nathan Wessler, an ACLU lawyer who has argued surveillance cases at the Supreme Court.

An Electronic Frontier Foundation investigation documented deputies in Texas using a nationwide plate reader network to search for a woman who had a self-managed abortion. Both searches were logged as “had an abortion, search for female.”

Each of these surveillance systems gets evaluated on its own, if at all. But no one is assessing what happens when they all feed into the same task forces.

Barry Friedman, a law professor at NYU, was direct: “No oversight. None.”

The drug strategy’s surveillance architecture has no special court, no sunset clause, no congressional debate – none of the safeguards that even counterterrorism surveillance has under the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act.

And the distinction between drug enforcement and counterterrorism may no longer hold.

The company positioned to run the data architecture where those missions converge is already inside the federal health system.

HHS has an agreement worth up to $90 million, making Palantir’s platform available across the department. The department’s later-installed Chief Information Officer, Clark Minor, previously spent more than a decade at Palantir and oversees health data infrastructure across HHS.

Minor’s financial disclosure shows he held between $1 million and $5 million in Palantir stock for more than five months after taking office before selling. President Trump made Palantir stock purchases totaling six figures, disclosed in a transaction report signed May 8, 2026, while his administration has awarded the company more than $1 billion in federal contracts.

The concern is not that one company holds federal contracts. It is that the government is expanding integrated data infrastructure while failing to set boundaries around medical privacy, law enforcement access, and political repurposing.

The administration’s 2026 Counterterrorism Strategy, also released in May, classifies fentanyl and its precursor chemicals as “Weapons of Mass Destruction,” merging drug enforcement with counterterrorism and opening access to the broader surveillance authorities that come with it.

“There’s a tremendous danger that you have the executive branch throwing together a whole bunch of stuff in an attempt to look tough,” Wessler said. “And it will be years potentially before judges are able to get involved.”

Maltz sees integrated data as a matter of public safety. Law enforcement and the intelligence community, he argues, have long needed systems that pull together data collected under court order, so agencies can map criminal networks rather than get buried in scattered records. “The data sets that these agencies are receiving from the providers pursuant to court orders and search warrants and things like that, it’s a tidal wave of data,” he said. “It’s impossible to go through it without having robust technology tools.”

But health data is not just another intelligence stream. It is generated when people seek care, fill prescriptions, get screened in pregnancy, use medication, receive addiction treatment, drive to clinics, or live in communities whose sewage is tested. Once those data streams are fused with law enforcement and counterterrorism tools, the question is not whether the system can find people. It is whether there is anything it is forbidden to find.

To some drug enforcement insiders, the answer is better oversight, not less technology. Maltz argues that the United States is falling behind sophisticated criminal networks and that agencies need modern tools to save lives. But public health and privacy experts see a surveillance architecture being assembled with no rules governing what it can do, and no limit on what it could eventually target.

Oliva saw a system capable of identifying and tracking virtually anyone. “You can get anybody with all of them for sure,” she said, “no matter how off the grid they think they are.”

Wessler called it “a completely unprecedented look at somebody’s medical history, their movements, their associations.”

The administration has already offered clues about who else could be targeted. The same counterterrorism strategy that classifies fentanyl as a WMD also identifies “violent secular political groups whose ideology is anti-American, radically pro-transgender, and anarchist” as a priority target, alongside cartels and Islamist terrorists. It promises to use “all the tools constitutionally available to us to map them at home, identify their membership,” and to “cripple them operationally before they can maim or kill the innocent.”

The drug strategy builds the tools: prescription databases that track testosterone and abortion pills, wastewater that can detect hormones, AI on medical records, and license plate readers. The counterterrorism strategy identifies who they’re targeting and authorizes pre-emptive action.

Oliva offered the clearest warning. “Just pick something that they’ve decided, that MAHA [‘Make America Healthy Again’] has decided is bad tomorrow, despite all the scientific evidence, boom.” The HPV vaccine and mRNA vaccines. Tylenol. Hormones. Stem cell therapies. “It never ends. It just continues.”

Dr. Céline Gounder a physician, epidemiologist, and medical journalist. Read her newsletter UNDERLYING CONDITIONS here.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: $50 Billion in Taxes Avoided by Tech Giants Driving the Data Center Boom
item.description: Alphabet, Amazon, and Meta – three tech giants who are driving the AI and data center boom across the United States – avoided paying $49.7 billion in taxes last year, according to a new report. At the same time, last year, the companies committed to spending a combined $250 billion on AI and data…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Alphabet, Amazon, and Meta – three tech giants who are driving the AI and data center boom across the United States – avoided paying $49.7 billion in taxes last year, according to a new report. At the same time, last year, the companies committed to spending a combined $250 billion on AI and data centers.

The shocking figures come from a new report from the Climate and Community Institute (CCI) that details how the government is facilitating the mass proliferation of data centers across the country.

The report – titled, “AI First: How the Federal Government is Prioritizing AI Over People and Planet” – reveals how the AI boom and data center build-up is not an inevitable process, but rather the result of choices by a select group of people, with little regard for what the populace thinks.

This facilitation appears in both subtle and blatant ways. For instance, the report points to, Donald Trump's 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, and his 2025 Big Beautiful Bill, as well as other tax cuts and subsidies, have freed these corporations from billions they otherwise would owe.

Jesse Goldstein, an associate professor of sociology at Virginia Commonwealth University and researcher at CCI, tells Zeteo it’s important to understand exactly what this tax avoidance does for these companies. “I think it’s helpful to switch to a language of tax expenditures and then ask, why is the US government spending close to $50 billion on these massive, and massively profitable corporations?” he says. “What is frustrating about these particular tax expenditures is that the only ‘plan’ that they seem to support is this AI-First fever dream where the interests and world making visions of the tech industry have effectively supplanted any more rational, democratic or popular industrial planning.”

As data centers have cropped up in communities all across the country, there has been organic and intense pushback nationwide. Just in 2025, local opposition – motivated by objections to pollution or public subsidies for the corporate projects – helped block or stall 48 projects worth $156 billion.

People are seeing through the mirage, Goldstein says. “I think it’s clear when you actually listen to what people from across the political spectrum are saying about this infrastructure and these technologies, that Americans’ rights, liberties, and way of life is being infringed upon by a state-backed industry that wants us to believe that mass surveillance, autonomous murder-machines, and the enshittification of our culture is somehow progress.”

AI data centers are warehouses filled with computers, servers, and data storage systems that serve as the backbone for AI computing and technology. They are specifically designed, and house cooling systems required to handle the particularly intensive processing that AI necessitates. The facilities require gargantuan amounts of water and electricity.

There are at least 4,100 data centers across the U.S., with another nearly 3,000 in development.

Along with their massive use of resources, they can generate significant carbon emissions and noise pollution. (A low grade mechanical hum emanates from these centers at all hours of the day, perturbing nearby residents.)

A 2024 study estimated that data centers had already generated more than 105 million tons of carbon emissions in 2023.

Now, a new Wired investigation found that just 11 data center campuses across the U.S. have the potential to generate 129 million tons of greenhouse gases – more than entire nations. Another study finds AI servers could have an annual water footprint of up to 1,125 million cubic meters – or, in other words, 297 billion gallons of water.

That’s almost 3 million American households’ worth of water.

One of Meta’s latest AI data center projects, the CCI report notes, demands the same amount of energy as 4 million homes – more than enough to power every home in the state of New Jersey.

All of that is why a whopping 70% of Americans oppose the idea of AI data centers being constructed in their areas.

Many cite environmental impact and excessive use of resources as their top concerns. While the powers that be insist that any delay in development would have the U.S. losing the AI “arms race,” majorities of voters across party lines support a data center moratorium – a temporary ban in construction of data centers until the U.S. can institute strong guardrails.

Despite all the costs and consequent mass resistance, the Trump administration has taken a series of steps to open the floodgates for the unpopular and destructive data centers.

AI moguls have ingratiated themselves across government, on both sides of the aisle. President Trump specifically has delivered extensively on behalf of the industry. In the first week of his second term, Trump signed an executive order “Removing Barriers to American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence.”

The administration launched a Department of Justice task force to combat state AI regulation, and has argued that the “growing patchwork of 50 different state regulatory regimes” threatens “to stifle innovation and jeopardize America’s lead in the AI race.” Trump also issued a national policy framework in March 2026 calling on the government to “remove barriers” for accelerated AI development.

In order to expand energy supply for AI data centers, the Trump administration has even ordered fossil fuel plants that were set to retire to instead continue to operate; the report finds that if Trump were to expand that mandate across all the carbon-polluting plants set to retire, it could result in an additional $3 billion in costs for ratepayers and 30,000 excess deaths.

The administration has framed AI as a cause of national necessity – a development that, no matter what, must be allowed to proceed unabated. Tech companies, as the CCI report lays out, have participated in the myth-making. In an antitrust case alleging search monopolization, Google said that, if they were subject to increased government regulation, that would “hamstring how we develop AI.”

“That would hold back American innovation at a critical juncture. We’re in a fiercely competitive global race with China for the next generation of technology leadership, and Google is at the forefront of American companies making scientific and technological breakthroughs,” the company wrote.

In similar fashion, Elon Musk’s xAI ran dozens of gas turbines without permits to power a massive data center. The NAACP sued under the Clean Air Act. And now Trump’s Justice Department is actively intervening to dismiss the case, arguing that the data center is critical to national security – because Musk’s AI bot Grok was used to help bomb Iran.

Matt Haugen, research and editorial manager of the CCI, tells Zeteo the “AI race” framing helps make the buildout seem “inevitable.” Tech companies and politicians can cite amorphous national security grounds as a strategy to ramp up technology “that mostly benefits the select few and hurts working people,” Haugen says in our video above.

The data center boom illustrates how the government picks winners, Haugen points out. Why not have a government direct all that energy towards an agenda that serves people and the planet?

That project, CCI posits, is Green Economic Populism. In response to a status quo where “democracy” has in actual practice been “a system where money talks and the rest of us are told to listen,” Goldstein says, green economic populism “is an attempt to introduce a return to a more meaningful and substantive concept of democracy where people and communities, not corporations and financiers, get to shape our future.” He pointed to the New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s governance as this vision in practice.

Haugen and his colleagues argue this “race” focused on market and geopolitical dominance is only one of many possibilities for AI. “Instead, we advocate for a ‘race’ toward a liveable world (ecologically and economically) where abundance is not measured in asset prices alone, but in a more capacious, multi-dimensional sense of what working people actually need and want.”

Watch the interview above with Matt Haugen to hear exactly how the federal government is actively facilitating the AI data center boom – and what an affirmative, alternative vision can look like in response.
